clean_story:
Molten metal rivers. Earth’s hidden smelters.

The term supervolcano metal vents refers to cracks in a volcano’s magma chamber where heavy metals—like gold, copper, and zinc—separate from molten rock like oil floating on water.

Most volcanoes spew lava or ash. Supervolcanoes do that and act like giant furnaces. Deep underground, their magma is so hot it dissolves metals from surrounding rock. When the pressure drops, these metals cluster into dense, liquid veins—like syrup sinking in a shaken soda. At Yellowstone, drill samples revealed copper deposits 10x richer than typical mines. In New Zealand’s Taupō Volcanic Zone, geologists found gold particles embedded in sulfur crystals, formed when superheated gases blasted through vent walls.

These vents don’t just hoard metal—they launch it. The 74,000-year-old Toba eruption scattered platinum-group metals across Southeast Asia, detectable today in ancient soil layers. Unlike normal mines, these deposits form in decades, not millennia.

Three related subjects are magma chemistry secrets, extreme deep-earth microbes, and asteroid mining comparisons.
